items.title: Chickenpox incubation period
items.contentHtml:
Incubation period of chickenpox

  • The incubation period of chickenpox is 10 to 21 days, with an average of 14 to 16 days; may be shortened in immunosuppressed patients or after in utero exposure.
  • Transmissibility begins 1 to 2 days before the rash eruption and is maintained until all lesions are crusted, generally 5 to 7 days after the onset of the rash.
  • In patients receiving varicella-zoster immunoglobulin (VZIG) the period Incubation period can last up to 28 days.

items.title: Varicella polymorphous rash
items.contentHtml:
Typical manifestation: polymorphic rash

  • The rash of chickenpox is polymorphic: macules, papules, vesicles on an erythematous base (“dewdrop on a rose petal”) and scabs coexist in different stages. evolutionary.
  • It begins in scalp, face and trunk with centripetal distribution (greater density in the trunk than in extremities).
  • intense itching is characteristic; The vesicles are umbilicate and rupture easily, leaving scabs that fall off without a scar if there is no superinfection.

items.title: Chickenpox pneumonia
items.contentHtml:
Serious complication: chickenpox pneumonia

  • varicella pneumonia is the most common visceral complication in adults and pregnant women, with an incidence of up to 1 in 400 cases in adults.
  • It typically presents 1 to 6 days after the rash with cough, dyspnea, high fever and hemoptysis; chest x-ray shows bilateral nodular infiltrates and late miliary calcifications.
  • First-line treatment is intravenous acyclovir at a dose of 10 mg/kg every 8 hours for at least 7 days; Mortality without treatment can reach 10-30%.

items.title: VZIG in exposed neonates
items.contentHtml:
VZIG immunoglobulin in exposed neonates

  • Varicella-zoster immunoglobulin (VZIG) is indicated in exposed neonates whose mother develops chickenpox in the period of 5 days before delivery until 2 days after birth.
  • It is also administered to premature newborns (age gestational <28 weeks or weight <1000 g) exposed, regardless of maternal history.
  • The dose is 125 U (1 vial) per 10 kg of weight, intramuscularly, ideally within 96 hours after exposure; if not available, prophylactic acyclovir may be used as an alternative.

items.title: Rubella rash and lymphadenopathy
items.contentHtml:
Rubella: maculopapular rash and lymphadenopathy

  • Rubella presents with a fine maculopapular rash that begins on the face and extends to the trunk and extremities in 24 hours, disappearing in 3 days (hence “3-day measles”). days”).
  • Retroauricular, occipital and posterior cervical lymphadenopathy is characteristic, which can precede the rash and persist for weeks.
  • Other findings: low fever (<39°C), arthralgias and petechiae in the soft palate (Forscheimer's sign), although it is not pathognomonic.

items.title: Congenital rubella syndrome
items.contentHtml:
Congenital rubella syndrome

  • Congenital rubella syndrome occurs due to maternal infection in the first trimester of pregnancy, with a risk of malformations of up to 85% if the infection occurs before week 12.
  • The virus causes direct damage through necrosis. cellular and viral persistence in fetal tissues, generating intrauterine growth delay and multiple congenital anomalies.
  • The diagnosis is confirmed with detection of anti-rubella immunoglobulin M (IgM) in neonatal blood or polymerase chain reaction (PCR) in pharyngeal swab and urine of the newborn born.

items.title: Congenital rubella triad
items.contentHtml:
Classic congenital rubella triad

  • The classical congenital rubella triad includes: congenital heart disease (most common patent ductus arteriosus and pulmonary stenosis), cataracts (uni or bilateral, often with microphthalmia) and sensorineural deafness (the most common of the three).
  • Other common findings: hepatosplenomegaly, thrombocytopenic purpura (“blueberry pie lesions”), psychomotor delay and radiolucent osteitis in metaphysis.
  • Deafness can be unilateral or bilateral and can be detected late; For this reason, universal hearing screening is recommended in all suspected newborns.

items.title: MMR MMR vaccine
items.contentHtml:
Prevention with MMR vaccine (MMR)

  • The MMR vaccine (MMR) contains live attenuated measles, rubella and mumps viruses; efficacy for rubella is >95% with one dose.
  • The standard regimen in most countries is two doses: the first at 12 months of age and the second between 4 and 6 years; in susceptible adults two doses separated by at least 4 weeks are administered.
  • Seroconversion is measured by anti-rubella immunoglobulin G (IgG); titers ≥10 IU/mL are considered protective.

items.title: Pregnancy vaccine contraindication
items.contentHtml:
Contraindication of vaccine in pregnancy

  • The triple viral vaccine (MMR) is contraindicated during pregnancy as it is a live attenuated virus vaccine with theoretical risk of fetal infection and congenital rubella syndrome.
  • It is recommended to avoid pregnancy for at least 4 weeks after vaccination; However, inadvertent vaccination during pregnancy is not an indication for interruption, since the real risk of malformations is very low (<2%).
  • In susceptible (seronegative) postpartum women the vaccine should be administered before hospital discharge, even if they are breastfeeding, as it is not contraindicated during breastfeeding.

items.title: Measles vs rubella
items.contentHtml:
Differential diagnosis: measles vs rubella

  • Measles presents with high fever (>40°C), cough, coryza, conjunctivitis and Koplik's spots (pathognomonic) on the oral mucosa before the rash; the rash is confluent maculopapular with a cephalic-caudal distribution.
  • Rubella presents low fever, prominent retroauricular/cervical lymphadenopathy and non-confluent maculopapular rash that disappears in 3 days; there are no Koplik spots.
  • Diagnostic key: serology by enzyme immunoassay (ELISA) for specific immunoglobulin M (IgM); Detection of nucleic acid by polymerase chain reaction (PCR) in pharyngeal swab confirms the acute case.

items.title: Rubella arthritis in adults
items.contentHtml:
Risk of rubella arthritis in adults

  • Rubella arthritis is more common in adult women (up to 70% of cases) than in children; It presents as symmetrical polyarthritis that affects small joints (hands, wrists, knees).
  • The mechanism is immunocomplex and usually appears 1 to 7 days after the rash; Arthritis may persist for weeks or months, but rarely becomes chronic.
  • Transient arthralgia is also observed in 10-30% of women vaccinated with the MMR vaccine, especially in the first postpubertal dose, due to the rubella strain.

items.foco: Isolation: droplets in chickenpox and rubella
items.title: Droplet isolation
items.contentHtml:
Isolation: droplets in chickenpox and rubella

  • Both chickenpox and rubella require droplet isolation (standard precautions + surgical mask when being <1 meter from the patient) and contact isolation in chickenpox due to the risk of transmission through skin lesions.
  • In chicken pox, the patient must remain isolated until all lesions are crusted over; in rubella, isolation is maintained for 7 days from the onset of the rash.
  • In hospital settings, patients with chickenpox should be in individual room with negative pressure if possible, and susceptible contacts should receive post-exposure prophylaxis (vaccine or varicella-zoster immunoglobulin (VZIG) depending on the case).
